Qs.Headline & Outlet Metadata:
Fill with **verifiable facts only**:
• Headline text
• Publication/outlet name
• Publication date (YYYY‑MM‑DD; "Unknown" if absent)
• Author(s) as credited
• Outlet section/category (e.g., Health, Lifestyle)
• Outlet ownership/funding (parent company, nonprofit, government‑run, etc.)

*Sample chain‑of‑thought:* “The piece is on WebMD (for‑profit, owned by Internet Brands) dated 2025‑03‑02, authored by Dr. Lisa Esposito…”
Qs.Author/Outlet Background:
Summarize bias‑relevant background **not in the article text**:
• Author’s professional history, beats, previous controversies, retraction record.
• Outlet’s editorial stance, fact‑check reliability scores, history of medical misinformation (if any).
If nothing notable found, state so explicitly.

*Sample chain‑of‑thought:* “WebMD’s revenue model relies on pharma ads; author previously wrote promotional content for supplement brands…”
Qs.Advice Summary & Target Audience:
In ≤3 sentences paraphrase the core medical advice *and* identify who it is aimed at (e.g., adults with Type‑2 diabetes, parents of infants, general public). Do **not** inject opinion; stick to information retrieval.

*Sample chain‑of‑thought:* “Article recommends daily 1 g omega‑3 for adults seeking to lower triglycerides; targets readers worried about heart health…”
Qs.Evidence Hierarchy Table:
Create a markdown table:
| # | Study / Source (short title) | Evidence Level (RCT, Meta‑analysis, Cohort, Case Report, Animal, Anecdote, None) | Sample Size / N | Directly Supports Advice? (Y/N) | Initial Quality (High/Med/Low) |
List every study or statistic the article cites.

*Sample chain‑of‑thought:* “Cites 2024 JAMA RCT with N=1,200 (High); also links to 2018 mouse study (Low)…”
Qs.Alignment with Authoritative Bodies:
Compare each main recommendation to current guidelines (CDC, WHO, USPSTF, American Heart Assoc., etc.). Use a markdown table:
| Guideline Body | Relevant Guideline Year | Aligns? (Y/Partial/N) | Notes |
If no guideline exists, mark "N/A".

*Sample chain‑of‑thought:* “WHO 2023 nutrition guideline makes no specific dosage recommendation → Partial.”
Qs.Risk/Benefit Balance & Uncertainties:
Provide bullet points:
• Documented benefits (magnitude, timeframe)
• Documented risks/adverse effects (severity, frequency)
• Uncertainties / evidence gaps
• Population subgroups affected differently

*Sample chain‑of‑thought:* “Benefit: ↓TG by 15 % after 6 mo; Risk: mild GI upset in 5 % of participants; Unknown long‑term effect on bleeding risk…”
Qs.Author/Expert Credentials Check:
List every person presented as a medical authority (author or quoted expert) with:
• Highest credential (MD, PhD, RD, etc.)
• Current affiliation & specialty
• Board certification status (if applicable)
• Past conflicts or sanction history (Y/N + brief note)
If credentials cannot be verified write “Unverified”.

*Sample chain‑of‑thought:* “Dr. Allen – MD, cardiologist, Yale School of Medicine faculty, board‑certified, no sanctions…”
Qs.Potential Conflicts of Interest / Promot:
Note any financial ties, sponsorships, affiliate links, or product mentions that could bias content. Include disclosures stated in the article and any external findings. If none are found, write “No COI detected”.

*Sample chain‑of‑thought:* “Article links to Amazon omega‑3 product with affiliate code; author consults for supplement brand…”
